hypothesis: AI agents prompt-configured to embody distinct ethical frameworks can generate morally coherent arguments that are philosophically indistinguishable from human arguments representing the same frameworks when evaluated by expert philosophers
null_hypothesis: AI-generated moral arguments will be significantly distinguishable from human-generated arguments representing equivalent ethical frameworks when evaluated by expert philosophers
alternative_hypothesis: AI agents can authentically embody moral frameworks with sufficient fidelity to pass philosophical evaluation
falsification_criteria: Expert philosophers can correctly identify AI vs. human arguments >70% of the time
AI arguments show systematic logical inconsistencies within frameworks
AI arguments fail to demonstrate contextual moral sensitivity
methodology: Blind evaluation by expert philosophers with formal consistency checks

variables.dependent: Philosophical evaluation scores and distinguishability rates

hypothesis: Large-scale synthetic citizen juries (n>1000 agents) will demonstrate emergent moral consensus patterns that correlate significantly (r>0.7) with outcomes from matched human citizen juries on equivalent moral dilemmas
null_hypothesis: There is no significant correlation between synthetic and human citizen jury outcomes on moral policy questions
alternative_hypothesis: Synthetic deliberation produces consensus patterns that approximate human deliberative democracy outcomes
falsification_criteria: Correlation coefficient between synthetic and human jury outcomes <0.5
Synthetic juries show systematic bias toward particular ethical frameworks
Consensus formation patterns follow algorithmic rather than deliberative logic
methodology: Parallel deliberation with matched human control groups and correlation analysis

hypothesis: Synthetic citizen juries reveal moral reasoning patterns and consensus dynamics at population scales (n>10,000) that are inaccessible through traditional deliberative methods, providing novel insights into collective moral judgment
null_hypothesis: Large-scale synthetic deliberation provides no additional insights beyond those available from smaller human deliberative groups
alternative_hypothesis: Population-scale synthetic deliberation reveals emergent properties of moral reasoning not observable in smaller groups
falsification_criteria: Large-scale patterns are reproducible in smaller synthetic groups
Patterns are artifacts of computational processing rather than genuine moral phenomena
Scaling degrades rather than enhances deliberative quality
methodology: Multi-scale analysis with pattern recognition and emergent property identification

variables.control: Moral dilemma complexity and agent framework distribution

hypothesis: Synthetic citizen juries function as effective democratic enhancement tools that increase the quality and reach of public deliberation without displacing human agency in democratic decision-making
null_hypothesis: Synthetic citizen juries either fail to enhance democratic processes or actively undermine human democratic participation
alternative_hypothesis: Synthetic deliberation enhances democratic capacity while preserving human sovereignty over moral and political decisions
falsification_criteria: Public acceptance of synthetic jury recommendations is consistently lower than human jury recommendations
Policymakers report decreased confidence in deliberative outcomes when AI enhancement is disclosed
Evidence of decreased citizen engagement in political processes following AI integration
methodology: Field studies with public acceptance surveys and policymaker interviews

variables.independent: Synthetic jury integration vs. traditional deliberation
variables.dependent: Democratic legitimacy measures and participation rates

hypothesis: The combination of validated individual AI moral reasoning (H1) and successful consensus formation (H2) will enable scalable democratic deliberation (H3) that enhances rather than replaces human democratic participation (H4)
methodology: Multi-phase validation with progressive integration testing

hypothesis: Successful validation of synthetic deliberative democracy hypotheses will establish a new theoretical framework bridging AI ethics, political science, and moral psychology
note: This represents the broader theoretical impact rather than a directly testable hypothesis
